questions.question: Explain what pointer aliasing is and why it is particularly important in microcontroller programming.
keyPoints: Pointer aliasing occurs when two or more pointers reference the same memory location
Important in microcontrollers due to direct hardware register access
Memory-mapped I/O requires precise pointer handling
Can cause unpredictable behavior leading to hardware malfunction

questions.question: What is the Strict Aliasing Rule in C? Explain why violating this rule leads to undefined behavior.
keyPoints: Only char* pointers can legally alias other types
Accessing objects through pointers of different types violates the rule
Compilers optimize assuming no aliasing, generating incorrect code on violation
Undefined behavior means program may crash, produce wrong results, or appear to work

questions.question: Explain the purpose of the 'restrict' keyword in C. How does it help with compiler optimizations?
keyPoints: restrict is a type qualifier promising only one pointer accesses the object
Compiler can cache values in registers without reloading
Enables better loop optimizations and code generation
Violating the promise leads to undefined behavior

questions.question: What is the difference between memcpy and memmove? When should each be used?
keyPoints: memcpy assumes no overlap between source and destination
memmove handles overlapping regions using internal buffer
Use memmove when regions might overlap; memcpy for guaranteed non-overlapping
memmove is slightly slower due to additional handling

questions.question: Explain the concept of union-based type punning and why it is considered a safer approach for type conversion.
keyPoints: Union allows different types to share same memory location
Writing to one member and reading another achieves type punning
C99 standard defines behavior for union access
Safer than direct pointer casting which may violate strict aliasing

questions.question: Why is the 'volatile' keyword essential when programming hardware registers in microcontrollers?
keyPoints: Hardware registers can change without software intervention
Volatile prevents compiler from caching values in registers
Ensures every read/write goes to actual memory location
Prevents aggressive optimizations that could cause incorrect hardware access

questions.question: Explain the problem of function parameter aliasing with an example.
keyPoints: When two pointer parameters point to same memory location
Modifications through one parameter may not reflect in the other
Example: void foo(int* a, int* b) where a and b may point to same location
Compiler may cache values assuming no aliasing, causing incorrect results
